food_name: Sambal Judes (Extra Spicy Red Chilli Sambal)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for sambal judes; closest parent is chili pepper sauce food product (FOODON_03301710).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Chilli sauces and pastes) is the closest defensible parent category for sambal judes; no exact sambal judes code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for sambal judes or Indonesian chilli sambal condiment.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical condiment serving of sambal judes is approximately 20 g (about one heaped tablespoon), served alongside a main dish such as martabak or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Peppers, hot chili, red, raw (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FDC ID 170931); adapted for sambal judes composition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Sambal judes is a fresh red chilli-based condiment with minimal additional ingredients (shallots, garlic, salt). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for raw hot red chilli peppers (FDC ID 170931), which represents the dominant ingredient. Minor adjustments for shallot and garlic content are within the margin of estimation. No direct sambal judes entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chilli and capsicum-based condiments; analogous estimate based on raw red chilli pepper GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sambal judes. GI is estimated at approximately 15 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database classification of fresh chilli peppers and capsicum-based condiments, which are consistently low-GI foods due to their low carbohydrate content, high water content, and high capsaicin and fibre content. Glycemic load per 20 g serving = (15 x 8.8 x 0.20) / 100 = 0.26, rounded to 0.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content in the base ingredient; added salt during preparation may increase sodium
hypertension.guidance: Sambal judes prepared from fresh red chillies has naturally low sodium (~9 mg per 100 g); however, individuals with hypertension should be mindful of added salt during preparation and limit portion size to a standard condiment serve of approximately 20 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~15) and low glycemic load per serving (~0.3)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sambal judes has a very low glycemic index (~15) and negligible glycemic load per condiment serving (~0.3), making it a suitable condiment for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard condiment portions; capsaicin in red chillies may also have modest insulin-sensitising effects per published literature.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and high vitamin C content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sambal judes is very low in saturated fat (0.04 g per 100 g) and contains no cholesterol, with a high vitamin C content (143.7 mg per 100 g) that may support vascular health; it is a cardiovascular-friendly condiment when used in standard serving sizes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal Judes is an extra-spicy Indonesian red chilli condiment (sambal) made primarily from fresh red chillies (cabai merah), typically blended or ground with shallots, garlic, salt, and sometimes lime juice. The name 'judes' in Indonesian colloquially means fierce or sharp-tempered, reflecting the intense heat level of this preparation. It is classified at spice level 4 in the context of the Smile Martabak restaurant in Singapore, indicating a very high capsaicin intensity. The texture is coarsely ground to smooth paste depending on preparation, with a vivid red colour, fiery heat, and a fresh, slightly acidic flavour profile. Nutritionally, sambal judes is low in calories (~40 kcal per 100 g), very low in fat (0.4 g per 100 g), and provides a meaningful amount of vitamin C (143.7 mg per 100 g) and potassium (322 mg per 100 g). Its glycemic index is very low (~15), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. The sodium content of the base ingredient is low, though added salt during preparation should be monitored. Sambal judes is vegan, gluten-free, and halal. It is commonly served as a side condiment alongside Indonesian dishes such as martabak, nasi goreng, and grilled meats. In the Singapore food delivery context, it is offered as an add-on condiment by Indonesian cuisine restaurants.

confidence_reason: No direct sambal judes entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw hot red chilli pepper data (FDC ID 170931), which represents the dominant ingredient. The adaptation is defensible given the minimal additional ingredients, but exact restaurant-specific preparation may vary. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database classifications for fresh chilli-based condiments.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw hot red chilli peppers (FDC ID 170931) as no direct sambal judes entry exists in primary regional databases. Minor upward adjustment to sodium_mg (from ~9 mg base to 9 mg reported) reflects minimal added salt assumption; restaurant preparation may use more salt. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA source for raw chilli peppers; naturally zero in this plant-based minimally processed condiment). 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients). 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero in fresh chilli peppers per USDA source; confirmed zero). 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero as this is a plant-based food). 6. GI is estimated at 15 (low) based on analogous fresh chilli pepper and capsicum condiment data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for sambal judes exists in the published literature. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all sourced from USDA FDC ID 170931 and are considered reliable estimates for the dominant ingredient. 8. This food is served by 1 restaurant (Smile Martabak - Funan) on field survey data at SGD 1.50. 9. Indonesian cuisine requires Indonesian local name per multilingual rules; satisfied by 'Sambal Judes' in local_names.indonesian.
